title: Communication & Presentation Skills
subtitle: Essential Vocabulary and Grammar for Aptis Speaking & Writing

sections.title: Communication & Presentation Skills
sections.quote: Speaking clearly, structuring ideas, and engaging your audience.

features.title: Speaking Fluency
features.description: Build confidence when presenting ideas in formal and informal settings.

features.title: Audience Engagement
features.description: Use body language and rhetorical devices to keep listeners interested.

features.title: Organizing Ideas
features.description: Structure speeches and presentations with clear openings, bodies, and conclusions.

features.title: Aptis Practice
features.description: Apply your language to speaking, reading, and writing tasks.

badge.title: APTIS ALIGNED
badge.text: This module develops B2-level communication skills for presentations, discussions, and formal responses.

tip.title: B2 Communication Tip
tip.text: Instead of saying "I think it is good," try "I believe it is highly effective" or "In my view, it offers several advantages."

sections.title: 1. Presentation Vocabulary
sections.description: Tap cards to flip and listen.

cards.definition: To give a formal talk to an audience, often in a professional or academic setting.
To capture and maintain the attention of listeners.
To look at the audience directly while speaking.
To speak smoothly and without unnecessary pauses.
To organize your thoughts in a logical order.
cards.translation: Sắp xếp ý tưởng rõ ràng

cards.definition: To respond effectively to audience questions or objections.

tip.title: Pro-Tip
tip.text: Strong presenters often use short signposting phrases such as "To begin with", "In addition", and "To conclude" to guide the audience.

sections.title: 2. Formal Alternatives
sections.description: Replace common words with more polished B2-level expressions.

practiceTasks.label: Practice Task
practiceTasks.text: Rewrite one sentence from your last presentation using at least one formal alternative from the table.

practiceTasks.label: Speaking Drill
practiceTasks.text: Say the sentence aloud twice: "The proposal is persuasive and easy to understand."

practiceTasks.label: Nuance Check
practiceTasks.text: Compare "explain" and "clarify" in a short context.

tip.title: Pro-Tip: Precision Matters
tip.text: "Clarify" is often used when you want to make something easier to understand, while "elaborate" suggests giving more detail.

sections.title: 3. Hedging and Signposting
sections.description: Use cautious language and clear transitions to sound more formal and natural.

points.title: Hedging
points.function: Softens opinions and makes statements more polite
examples: I would say that this approach is effective.
It seems that audience engagement improves when visuals are used.

points.title: Signposting
points.function: Shows the structure of a talk or argument
examples: To begin with, I would like to explain the main goal.
In addition, the data suggests that the strategy works.

points.title: Transition Phrases
points.function: Helps the audience follow your ideas smoothly
examples: On the other hand, we should also consider the costs.
To conclude, the benefits outweigh the difficulties.

rows: I would say that this is a reasonable solution.
To begin with, I will explain the problem.
In addition, the survey shows strong support.

tip.title: Academic Pro-Tip
tip.text: In formal speaking, hedging helps you sound thoughtful rather than overly absolute.

sections.title: 4. Choose the Best Option
sections.description: Select the most appropriate word or phrase for each sentence.

questions.prompt: To make a strong first impression, you should ________ your main point clearly.
During a presentation, it is important to ________ the audience by using examples and questions.
If you want to sound more formal, you can say "________" instead of "I think".
A good introduction should ________ the audience and present the topic.
When you want to add another point, you can say "________".
A presenter should try to ________ eye contact with the audience rather than looking at the floor.
If a speaker wants to sound more polite, they may use the phrase "________".
At the end of a presentation, a speaker often ________ the main ideas briefly.

tip.title: Sentence Upgrade
tip.text: Original: "I think the plan is good." Upgrade: "I would say that the plan is quite effective and likely to succeed."

sections.title: 5. Match the Phrases
sections.description: Drag each phrase into the correct presentation stage.

items.text: To begin with, I would like to introduce the topic.
In addition, the research shows clear evidence.
To conclude, I would like to summarize the key points.
On the other hand, there are some limitations to consider.
First of all, let me explain the context.

tip.title: Presentation Structure
tip.text: A clear structure helps the audience follow your message and improves your overall impact.

sections.title: 6. Reading Practice: A Good Presentation
sections.description: Complete the text with the best words from the box.

passage.title: Useful Habits for a Strong Presentation
parts: A successful presentation begins with a clear
the audience by using stories, questions, or examples. It is also helpful to
important points in simple language so that everyone understands. During the talk, the presenter should speak

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of the passage?
options: To explain how to prepare a presentation

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what should a speaker do when answering difficult questions?

tip.title: Reading Tip
tip.text: Pay attention to collocations such as "engage the audience" and "clarify important points".

sections.title: 7. Write a Short Presentation Response
sections.description: Practice writing a clear, structured response for an Aptis-style task.

prompt.situation: You have been asked to give a short presentation about a skill that is important for students today. Describe the skill, explain why it matters, and give one example.
prompt.placeholder: Write about a useful skill such as communication, teamwork, or digital literacy.
prompt.referenceAnswer: Communication is an essential skill for students because it helps them express ideas clearly and work well with others. In my view, it matters in both academic and professional life. For example, a student who can explain a project confidently is more likely to succeed in group work and interviews.

tip.title: Writing Tip
tip.text: Try to use a simple structure: introduction, main point, example, and conclusion.
